narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and an LSAT score of 32, your early academic deciles of 3rd in both Year 1 and Year 3 placed you below the typical student's standing, while your upper-middle family income and plan to attend full-time aligned more closely with the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, which evaluates law school candidates based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicted a 48.2% chance that you would fail it—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes students with stronger projected bar success. This prediction wasn't random; it stemmed from a careful weighing of your profile against patterns seen in thousands of past students. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most influential, actually working in your favor by suggesting a better-than-expected shot at passing the bar compared to what the model anticipated from similar profiles. However, your Year 3 decile rank pushed strongly against you, as being in the lower third of your class signaled a heightened risk of bar failure to the model, more so than for the average student who lands around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score followed closely as the third-biggest driver, contributing toward the failure prediction because it fell notably below the dataset average of 36.7, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness. Overall, these top three elements—your GPA helping to offset risks, but your later decile and LSAT amplifying them—overrode other strengths like your full-time commitment, which is common among 93% of students and didn't sway the outcome much. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher GPAs, LSATs, and class rankings, your profile clustered more with those who struggled on the bar. This doesn't define your potential, but it explains the model's cautious stance. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your class rank through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth since undergrad. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value comebacks like yours. Keep pushing—you've already shown upper-middle stability that many lack.
